news_communities.title: Man sentenced to life in prison for 2023 killing of girlfriend in Chelsea
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Staff Reports
news_communities.summary:
A man who pleaded guilty to stabbing his estranged girlfriend to death at her apartment in Chelsea, Massachusetts, in 2023 was sentenced to life in prison on Monday.

Angel Alvarez, 68, was charged with killing 59-year-old Margarita Morehead in May 2023. He was arrested hours after Morehead was found fatally stabbed on the floor of her apartment on LaFayette Avenue on May 8.

The pair had dated for several years, and Alvarez had lived on and off in Morehead’s home. At the time of the killing, Morehead had a restraining order out against Alvarez. Prosecutors said that Alvarez was frequently jealous of her and hostile, accusing her of cheating on him.

Alvarez pleaded guilty to second-degree murder, assault and battery with a dangerous weapon and violation of protection order on February 11. He was sentenced to life in prison with the possibility of parole after 17 years.

If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic violence, contact the National Domestic Violence Hotline by calling 1-800-799-SAFE (7233), visiting www.thehotline.org or texting LOVEIS to 22522.

On the morning of her death, Alvarez was at Morehead’s apartment and they were arguing over her attempt to leave him, prosecutors said.

Prosecutors said Morehead punched Alvarez that day and when she told him her daughter would see the injuries, he became enraged. Alvarez then grabbed a kitchen knife and stabbed Morehead multiple times in the chest, cutting off two of his own fingers. He then left, leaving Morehead to bleed out on the floor.

A neighbor heard Morehead’s screams and saw a man, later identified as Alvarez, walk out, leaving a trail of blood, but he didn’t call for help, prosecutors said. Instead, after the better part of an hour, his partner called a domestic violence hotline, and a staff member there called 911.

Morehead was found unresponsive, with the kitchen knife near her. Prosecutors said Alvarez bought new clothes at a Target, where his bloody old clothes were found, and told others that he had stabbed Morehead and was thinking about harming himself. He turned himself in that evening, allegedly at his family’s advice.

Morehead was a mother of four. Her children spoke during the sentencing hearing.

Our mother, Margarita, was not defined by the way her life ended, but by the way she lived, by the love she gave to family, the guidance she offered to others, and the quiet strength she carried every day,” one of her sons told the court.

“Domestic violence didn’t just take my mother’s life. It took a grandmother from my son, a parent from me, and a piece of our family that can never be replaced,” her daughter added.

news_communities.title: Man convicted of killing Salem State student in 1986
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A man was found guilty Tuesday in the 1986 murder of a Massachusetts college student.

John Carey, 66, was serving a 20-year sentence for trying to kill his neighbor by choking her in 2007 when he was charged with the murder of 20-year-old Claire Gravel of North Andover.

Gravel, a student at Salem State College, was strangled with her tank top. Prosecutors with the Essex County District Attorney’s Office said a breakthrough came when testing found samples of Carey’s DNA on the garment.

During closing arguments in Essex County Superior Court Monday, Assistant District Attorney Kim Faitella told jurors, “he forcefully grabbed Claire’s tank top and twisted it and strangled her to death to satisfy his sexual interest in strangulation.”

Gravel’s body was found in the woods in Beverly a day after she had been at a pub in Salem.

“The family of Claire Gravel has waited 40 long years for justice,” District Attorney Paul Tucker said in a statement Tuesday. “The prosecutors here today, together with our partners in law enforcement, never gave up on Claire’s case and today we are pleased that at least the family has some answers – some closure.”

Carey was previously convicted of the attempted murder of Rosemary Diskin, whom he choked inside her Hamilton home.

His defense attorney, Mark Booker, said in court Monday that jurors “should find Mr. Carey not guilty,” arguing that police failed to conduct a thorough investigation in 1986 and pointing to what he described as missing records and inconsistencies in witness statements over time.

Carey is due to be sentenced on March 26.

news_communities.title: Case against man charged in 1986 murder of Salem student goes to jury
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain
news_communities.summary:
Update March 3: John Carey has been convicted in the murder of Claire Gravel. Read the latest here.

A Massachusetts jury began deliberations Monday in the decades-old killing of a Salem State College student, weighing whether a man — already serving time for a separate strangulation attack — is responsible for her death.

Prosecutors with the Essex County District Attorney’s Office allege that John Carey, 66, murdered 20-year-old Claire Gravel of North Andover in 1986. Gravel’s body was found in the woods in Beverly a day after she had been at a pub in neighboring Salem. Witnesses said she was last seen being dropped off at her apartment in Salem the previous night.

During closing arguments in Essex County Superior Court, Assistant District Attorney Kim Faitella told jurors, “he forcefully grabbed Claire’s tank top and twisted it and strangled her to death to satisfy his sexual interest in strangulation.”

Carey is already serving a 20-year sentence for attempting to kill a neighbor Rosemary Diskin in Hamilton in 2007 by choking her inside her home.

Defense attorney Mark Booker urged jurors to reject the prosecution’s case, telling them, “You should find Mr. Carey not guilty.”

Booker argued investigators failed to conduct a thorough investigation in 1986 and pointed to what he described as missing records and inconsistencies in witness statements over time.

Despite the presence of DNA evidence, legal analysts say the case is not necessarily straightforward. The defense must only raise reasonable doubt, particularly in a case built in part on decades-old witness testimony.

“All the defendant has to do is create that reasonable doubt,” said NBC10 Boston legal analyst Michael Coyne. “And when you’re dealing with witnesses, memories and statements over such a long period of time, there will be differences between what the trial testimony is and what it was at one point, what their witness statement said.”

Jurors are expected to continue deliberating until they reach a verdict.

news_communities.title: New details revealed in court about Malden teacher accused of raping high school student
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_communities.summary:
A 54-year-old teacher accused of raping a high school student in Malden, Massachusetts, faced a judge Thursday, a day after his arrest.

Authorities allege Scott Marino gave alcohol to an 18-year-old who is known to him and then sexually assaulted her last Wednesday, Feb. 18, at his Stoneham home, where the victim was currently living. Marino was arrested a week after the alleged incident and has been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under the age of 21, according to the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office.

Prosecutors provided shocking new details Thursday in Woburn District Court at the arraignment for Marino, who teaches at Linden STEAM Academy in Malden and coaches the girls’ basketball team at Malden High School.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Jacob McCrindle of the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office said in court that the alleged victim in this case had been in the foster care of Marino and his wife at the time this occurred. The victim, who moved in with the Marinos at their Stoneham home in the fall, was a basketball player for Marino during the season, and the two were known to each other for many years.

According to prosecutors, Marino’s wife was scheduled to take a trip out of state last Wednesday. The victim was on school vacation, so she went with Marino and a grandfather who lives in a basement apartment at the home to an Ash Wednesday mass. They dropped the older man off at home, and then Marino and the victim went driving because she had a learner’s permit and was learning to drive on the highway. They made the decision to go into Boston to get a takeout seafood dinner, and on the way home, Marino had them stop at a liquor store where surveillance video shows he purchased a strawberry margarita mix, a large bottle of Patron tequila and some other agave liquor.

The two then went back to Marino’s Stoneham home and he allegedly gave her a large margarita. She drank some of it but didn’t like it, though Marino allegedly encouraged her to keep drinking. He later poured them shots of tequila, and they ate the seafood dinner they got. The victim later told police that she was feeling ill so she went to her bedroom where she vomited many times, eventually lying down in bed.

Marino then came in and sexually assaulted her two times, prosecutors said in court. The victim later retrieved her cell phone and began texting friends and ultimately an aunt, begging for help.

McCrindle said that the aunt called 911 as she was driving to the Marino’s home, and the victim was taken to Winchester Hospital for a full sexual assault examination.

“This is a relationship where the defendant was the victim’s foster parent, he took her into his home, that’s a position of trust, as well as coupled with the fact that the defendant is a teacher, or was a teacher at the Malden Public Schools and he was also her coach for many years,” McCrindle said in court, arguing for $100,000 cash bail. “The defendant used I would suggest that relationship, he took advantage of that relationship, there’s a significant age difference, the defendant is 54 years old, the victim is 18 years old. There’s also a significant size difference that’s described in the police report between the defendant and the alleged victim…. it’s also noteworthy that the defendant appears to have waited until his wife left the home so he was more isolated with the victim…clearly some planning went into this.”

Marino’s defense attorney argued before the judge that he’s a respected educator with deep community support who has “never been in court in his life.”

The judge ultimately ordered $75,000 cash bail based on the gravity of circumstances along with several conditions requested by the commonwealth, including that Marino stay away and have no contact from the victim and any witnesses, stay away from all Malden school property and events, be fitted with a GPS bracelet to enforce exclusive zones including the victim’s home address, her school, her workplace and a gym that she frequents, surrender his passport and not be allowed to leave the state.

The judge also added the condition that Marino be alcohol free and submit to SCRAM testing.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Malden Superintendent Timothy Sippel previously said the district was informed Wednesday that Marino had been taken into custody in connection with “a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

Marino was immediately placed on leave, the superintendent added, noting that the district is cooperating fully with law enforcement agencies in their investigation.

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” Sippel said in a statement. “The safety of our students and staff is our highest priority.”

A plea of not guilty was entered on Marino’s behalf. He is due back in court for a probable cause hearing on April 16.

A teacher and coach in Malden, Massachusetts, has charged with the rape of an 18-year-old.

Malden Public Schools said Scott Marino had been arrested over a “serious incident” involving a high school student. The Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office and Stoneham Police Department later said he had been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under 21.

Marino is accused of providing alcohol to the victim, who is known to him and sexually assaulting her on Feb. 18, prosecutors said.

Superintendent Timothy Sippel said Marino teaches at Linden STEAM Academy — which serves kindergarten through eighth grade — and is the head coach of the Malden High School girls’ basketball team.

Sippel said that Marino’s “arrest is in connection with a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” the superintendent said.

Marino is due to be arraigned Thursday in Woburn District Court. It was not immediately clear if he had an attorney.

news_communities.title: Girl rescued after falling through the ice in Malden
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Firefighters rescued a teen girl who had fallen through the ice into the frigid water of a pond in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday afternoon.

It happened around 2 p.m. at Fellsmere Pond, fire officials said. Firefighters in water rescue suits went in to find the girl holding onto the edge of the ice, according to the Malden Fire Department. She was pulled from the water in under 30 seconds.

“I don’t even think the engine stopped,” Malden Fire Captain Roy Tribble said. “I jumped up, I sprinted down here, pretty much just jumped right into the water.”

The 14-year-old girl was conscious and alert when she was brought to a Boston hospital for treatment.

Tribble said the water was about 30 feet deep.

“A life was saved. It was a young girl who fell through the ice,” added Captain Bob Hegarty.

He warned everyone to stay off thin ice.

“A warning to everybody out there that ice, if it’s not thick enough, you’re going to fall through, and if you fall through, you’re not going to be able to get yourself out,” Hegarty said. “So stay off the ice until it’s very thick, which — who can tell when it’s very thick?”

news_communities.title: Malden woman accused of threatening federal agents making immigration arrest
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts woman is accused of threatening to kill federal agents who were conducting immigration enforcement in Malden.

Bethany Abigail Terrill, 37, was charged with threatening a United States official, the U.S. Attorney’s Office said Thursday.

Prosecutors say that Terrill approached the agents as they were making an arrest outside Malden District Court on Sept. 29, describing her as verbally abusive. She allegedly shouted things like “ICE is here, ICE is here,” “You guys are monsters, this is insane,” “Sir, what’s your name, what’s your name,” “I can try to help you,” and “I am an American civilian, I have a right to be here,” and called the agents Nazis.

She is further accused of trying to push past the agents, yelling “Charlie Kirk died, and we love it… We’re coming for you, gonna kill you.” 

According to the statement of probable cause, an agent heard the threat and proceeded to detain Terrill, who they said was “actively resistant and fought to escape.”

Officials say the agents were wearing body cameras, and Terrill was also filming at the time. In the statement of probable cause, investigators say the threat can be heard in the footage from Terrill’s phone.

The agents on scene were from multiple agencies, including Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), ICE and the FBI.

During her appearance in U.S. District Court in Boston on Thursday, a public defender said Terrill owns a salon and was struggling with legal issues in an unrelated case.

Judge Paul Levenson ordered Terrill be released with conditions, saying “federal agents have a right to not be subjected to threats.”

Terrill was ordered as part of her conditions of release to participate in a mental health program for treatment on a regular basis, to develop a safety plan for her own safety and others, avoid contact with potential witnesses, have no contact with any federal official, family or otherwise in any way or form, refrain from alcohol and the use of any illegal drugs, to avoid contact with law enforcement, and to refrain from making any further threats.

She didn’t speak to reporters outside of court.

The charge of threatening a U.S. official carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine of $250,000.

news_communities.title: Cities bordering Everett unite in Revolution soccer stadium transparency push
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Four cities that border Everett, Massachusetts, have banded together in a push for more involvement in the New England Revolution’s plans for a soccer-specific stadium in the city.

The mayors of Revere, Medford, Malden and Chelsea formed a coalition called City Mayors for Citizens that they said will seek transparency on plans for the stadium, including the impacts it will have on their communities.

The 25,000-seat stadium proposal has progressed in the state Legislature but still faces plenty of hurdles. The City Mayors for Citizens said they are worried about how the building on along the Mystic River might impact local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life.

“A project of this size and scale will have significant regional impacts,” said Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe Jr said in a statement. “We want to ensure that a stadium is contributing positively to the communities it is drawing resources from. Our residents should not bear the burdens of a private stadium that has strong potential to negatively affect their day-to-day lives.”

Asked for a response to the statement, a Revolution club spokesperson noted that it expects to work with neighboring communities after it reaches agreements with Everett and Boston, which would allow the property to have its port designation removed and the process of construction to continue.

The spokesperson also noted that the team thanked the mayors and promised to review the concerns raised in their letter, sent Monday.

news_communities.title: Video shows Salvadoran man being detained by ICE in Malden
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain, Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
The wife of a Salvadoran man says he was violently taken into custody by immigration officials on his way to work in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning.

The incident occurred shortly before 6 a.m. in the area of 28 Kennedy Drive.

Leslie Perlera Gonzalez said she received a distressing call from her husband, Edgar Hernan Elias Escobar, shortly before 6 a.m. as he was heading to his construction job. She rushed to Kennedy Drive, where she said Elias Escobar was surrounded by immigration officers.

“I heard them smash his window, and he was saying, ‘Ow, you’re hurting me,'” Perlera Gonzalez said. “When I arrived on the scene, he had a Taser on his face.”

The 31-year-old had no criminal record and was in the process of adjusting his immigration status after the couple married last year, noted Perlera Gonzalez.

“He’s never come close to doing anything that would be considered a crime,” she said.

She alleged that agents used an unmarked car to ram her husband’s vehicle, forcing him off the road before detaining him.

Malden police said Elias Escobar sustained minor injuries. He was transported on a stretcher to a hospital in Melrose, where Perlera Gonzalez said she was not allowed to see him.

About an hour later, he was moved to a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing facility in Burlington.

A spokesperson from the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement that Elias Escobar was the target of the ICE operation and suffered “no injuries.”

“On September 17, 2025, ICE conducted an immigration enforcement operation in Malden, Massachusetts to arrest Edgar Hernan Elias-Escobar, an illegal alien from El Salvador. During the arrest, the illegal alien refused to comply with officer commands to rolldown his window. The officers took appropriate action and followed their training to use the minimum amount of force necessary to resolve the situation in a manner that ensured the success of the operation and prioritized the safety of the public and our officers,” the spokesperson said in a statement. “Out of an abundance of caution, ICE law enforcement escorted Elias-Escobar to the hospital for evaluations. Medical staff found he had no injuries and discharged him. He was then escorted to the Boston Filed office for processing and will be placed in removal proceedings.”

“I’ve been expecting this from somebody I know every day since Donald Trump was elected,” said Amiee Parco, an attorney with Greater Boston Legal Services and a colleague of Perlera Gonzalez. “I’ve been waiting for it to affect me personally.”

Friends and coworkers gathered outside the Burlington facility, joining a weekly protest to support Elias Escobar’s release and that of other detainees.

“He’s a really wonderful person,” said Alyssa Rao, a friend of the couple. “I think this is going to impact everyone.”

After Wednesday’s ordeal, Perlera Gonzalez fears what her husband is going through while detained.

“I’m terrified of what could be happening when no one is watching,” she said.

At last check, Escobar was still at the facility. It’s unclear how long he’ll be held — often, people stay several days, though the facility is meant to detain people for hours.

His lawyer is trying to prevent the government from sending him to a far-away detention center, and from deportation.

ICE has ramped up arrests across greater Boston in recent weeks under “Operation Patriot 2.0,” detaining immigrants in public spaces.

Gov. Maura Healey spoke out against the sweeps on Tuesday, saying federal immigration enforcement officers are “creating an incredible amount of fear and intimidation.”

“Some of it seems purposeful, to be honest. Why else are you parked outside a church? Why are you parked outside a community center?”

The governor said her office has received no information from ICE about who is being detained or why.

“This is not a furtherance of public safety,” she said. “In fact, it undermines public safety.”

Asked what she can do to protest White House policy, Healey said: “I join with other governors in urging the Trump administration, in the strongest possible terms, to change course.”

State House News Service contributed to this report.

ICE crackdowns intensify across Boston as sanctuary cities face Trump's latest operation

news_boston.title: Shooting reported at hotel in Sturbridge
news_boston.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_boston.summary:
There was a large police response to a hotel in Sturbridge, Massachusetts, following a report of a shooting overnight.

Sturbridge police confirm they responded along with Massachusetts State Police to 400 Haynes Street, the listed address for the Sturbridge Plaza Hotel, around 12:55 a.m. Thursday.

Video from the scene showed a black SUV had crashed into the hotel and appeared lodged in a room of the building. Investigators were seen looking inside of the vehicle, and walking around it with a flashlight.

It’s unclear if anyone was hurt in this incident.

Police said on Facebook just before 6 a.m. that there is no threat to the public at this time.

Few other details have been released. Police said additional information is being gathered and asked the public to avoid speculation, allowing investigators the time necessary to conduct a thorough and complete investigation.

The Worcester County District Attorney’s Office is involved in that investigation, which remains active and ongoing.

news_boston.author: Dominic Brown
news_boston.summary:
You couldn’t have asked for better weather here in Boston over the past few days, with all the sunshine we’ve seen and the warm air we’ve felt. But as the old saying goes, all good things must come to an end – and it’s ending today!

Weather alerts

Click here for any active weather alerts.

Strong to severe storms possible today; brief isolated tornado can’t be ruled out

In fact, today is a First Alert! Strong to severe storms could impact parts of Greater Boston this afternoon and evening.

A frontal system to our west will bring scattered showers to the region this morning.

But later this afternoon, a few strong to severe storms could develop, capable of producing damaging winds, small hail and periods of heavy rain.

A brief isolated tornado cannot be ruled out in our region today.

But let’s be clear — the threat for severe storms will be contingent upon sunshine today.

In fact, if our clouds break a bit to see some sunshine after the morning rain, we could have a few rowdy thunderstorms in parts of central and eastern Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire. If we don’t see as much sunshine today, our overall threat for severe weather will be lowered. So, we’ll have to keep an eye on the radar today.

Showers and storms will likely diminish after dinnertime tonight. Regardless of the rain, though, we’ll all likely have gusty winds, with gusts up to 40 mph at times.

High temperatures will be in the 70s to near 80 in spots. Low temperatures will be in the 60s.

Live radar

Flood watch, wind advisory

There are flood watches in parts of New Hampshire and Maine from noon today through this evening. Flash flooding caused by excessive rainfall from thunderstorms is possible.

There are also wind advisories in Vermont in effect from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. Wind gusts up to 50 mph are expected.

Friday forecast plus weekend weather

The sun returns for Juneteenth this Friday and again on Saturday. Each day will be a bit breezy. Highs will be in the low to mid 80s.

A few late day storms are possible on Father’s Day this Sunday, followed by scattered showers on Monday.

news_boston.title: What do Iran and the United States stand to gain from their deal? Here's what to know
news_boston.author: Lee Keath | The Associated Press
news_boston.summary:
The interim deal reached by the United States and Iran to end their war will reopen the Strait of Hormuz and bring the two adversaries back to the negotiating table over Tehran’s nuclear program, according to details released by both countries. It will also give Iran an immediate benefit, allowing it to sell its oil freely again.

Besides the new oil revenue for Iran, the two sides are more or less back where they were 3½ months ago — before Israel and the U.S. on Feb. 28 launched their war on Iran, which has left thousands dead across the region, triggered a global energy crisis and shaken the American economy.

Iran and the U.S. will enter a 60-day period of negotiations, and hanging over them will be the question of whether President Donald Trump can wrest a better deal than the 2015 nuclear accord he scuttled eight years ago.

Here’s what to know based on details released by U.S. officials and Iranian state media:

The interim deal would get the oil flowing again

Trump and Vice President JD Vance signed the agreement digitally over the weekend and Trump signed a physical copy Wednesday while dining with French President Emmanuel Macron in the Palace of Versailles.

In Tehran, a stone-faced President Masoud Pezeshkian signed the deal on behalf of Iran, according to the state-run IRNA news agency, which posted an image of him holding up the deal with his signature and Trump’s.

Under the agreement, the Strait of Hormuz will reopen and the U.S. will lift its blockade of Iranian ports, which should push gas prices down. Passage through the waterway will be toll-free for only 60 days, and the deal doesn’t preclude fees in future, according to U.S. officials who spoke on the condition of anonymity to read details of the draft, which has not yet been officially released by Washington.

Iran’s closure of the strait, through which around a fifth of the world’s traded oil supplies transited toward the open ocean before the war started, proved perhaps its strongest weapon. It drove up global fuel prices, made food and other basics like fertilizer more expensive, and helped push U.S. inflation to 4% ahead of this fall’s midterm elections.

War with Iran 16 hours ago

Trump and Iran's president digitally sign MOU with terms to end war

Trump Administration Jun 17

G7 leaders back Trump's deal to end Iran war as more details of it emerge

Iran will be able to sell oil freely

The deal immediately waives, but doesn’t eliminate, sanctions that Trump imposed on Iran’s oil exports, allowing it once again sell its crude on the world market and restoring a revenue stream worth billions.

Last year, Iran earned an estimated $45 billion from oil sales. But it had only one major buyer, China, and had to ship its crude through a shadow fleet of tankers to elude sanctions, eating into its profits. Under the blockade since April, its exports have nearly ground to a halt.

With the waiver, Iran will likely be able to find more customers and sell its oil for higher market prices.

Iran got promises for the future

The draft agreement includes language on Iran’s highly enriched uranium, calling for it to be “downblended” to a lower purity under supervision of the International Atomic Energy Agency, without elaborating. But negotiations on the particulars of Tehran’s nuclear program still lie ahead.

Trump withdrew from the previous nuclear deal in 2018, saying it gave a huge windfall to Iran and calling it “the worst deal ever.” But the interim deal outlines even more lucrative incentives for Iran if it reaches a new agreement with the U.S. on its nuclear program.

One is the eventual lifting of all international sanctions, which would seem to go further than the 2015 accord. That agreement lifted sanctions related to Iran’s nuclear program but kept others in place over what the U.S. alleged were Tehran’s support for terrorism and rights abuses.

The interim pact also promises a $300 billion fund for reconstruction of Iran’s war damage. Vance has said Gulf Arab nations would invest that amount. But Gulf countries would likely be reluctant to help Iran after Iranian attacks in the war destroyed oil facilities and other sites in their territory.

Trump reiterated Wednesday that the U.S. would not contribute and said it was up to other countries if they wanted to invest.

To give a sense of the extraordinary scale of the fund, the World Bank estimates that Syria, after 13 years of destructive civil war, needs $215 billion for reconstruction; the Gaza Strip, largely flattened in two years of war between Israel and Hamas, needs $53 billion.

The deal also promises to unfreeze billions of dollars worth of Iranian assets held abroad during the negotiations under a procedure the two sides will work out, according to the text provided by U.S. officials.

Iran’s missiles and proxies don’t seem to be on the table

The Trump administration said its war aims were to “obliterate” Iran’s missile arsenal, “sever its support” for proxies in the region, “annihilate its navy” and ensure it never acquires a nuclear weapon.

The seven weeks of U.S.-Israeli bombardment are believed to have heavily damaged Iran’s missile arsenal and production facilities as well as other parts of its military. How heavily isn’t known, though, and Iran continued to fire on Israel as recently as last week. Meanwhile, Iran’s ties with its militant proxies — Hezbollah in Lebanon, the Houthis in Yemen and Shiite militias in Iraq — appear strong as ever.

Neither the missile issue nor Iran’s support for its allies appears to be on the table in the upcoming negotiations. The interim deal only specifies that the talks will focus on Iran’s nuclear program.

War in Lebanon could threaten the deal

The deal calls for an end to the war in Lebanon, where Israel has been fighting Hezbollah.

However, Israel and Hezbollah aren’t parties to the agreement. Iran insists Israel must withdraw from the large swath of southern Lebanon it has occupied since March, but the interim deal doesn’t explicitly require that and only affirms a commitment to ensuring Lebanon’s “territorial integrity.”

Israel has vowed to keep its troops in the zone, while Hezbollah says it is committed to resisting Israel “until full withdrawal is achieved.” If fighting spirals, it could derail the U.S.-Iran deal unless the two countries can rein in their respective allies.

U.S.-Israeli ties have been strained

Israel was squeezed out of the negotiations with Iran, and Israelis from across the political spectrum have called the deal a disaster, directing their fury at Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

Trump, meanwhile, has grown more scathing in his displeasure with Netanyahu, even describing him as “crazy.” During the negotiations with Iran, Trump was furious over Israel’s strikes in Beirut, warning they could jeopardize an agreement.

In France on Tuesday, Trump said at the annual G7 summit that “without the U.S., there would be no Israel,” and added that Netanyahu “has to be more responsible with respect to Lebanon.”

Netanyahu is left in a precarious situation ahead of national elections later this year. His relationship with Trump may require downscaling a military campaign in Lebanon that is widely popular in Israel.

Meanwhile, Israel’s arch-nemesis, Iran, would emerge from the war seemingly bolder.

The Islamic Republic survived the most serious attempt ever by Israel and the United States to topple it, despite their thundering opening volleys of the war that killed Iran’s supreme leader and other top officials. And Iran demonstrated its ability to retaliate economically by shutting down the strait and striking U.S. Arab allies in the Gulf, giving Tehran confidence that Trump won’t seek a return to war.

Much depends on the final agreement

The 2015 agreement negotiated by the Obama administration severely limited Iran’s nuclear program for 15 years. During that period, Iran could only enrich uranium to a low level, 3.67%, which is far below the 90% needed for a weapon. It could only stockpile 300 kilograms of the material and had to sharply reduce its centrifuges carrying out enrichment. It was also put under stricter inspections by the U.N.’s nuclear watchdog agency.

One main criticism was the 15-year time limit, after which opponents said Iran would be able to quickly ramp up its ability to produce a bomb.

A key question now will be whether the U.S. can win stricter limits on Iran’s program for a longer term. The United States wants Iran to give up or dilute its stockpile of highly enriched uranium, which Iran developed in retaliation after Trump pulled out of the 2015 accord.

Even if Iran agrees to that, it is almost certain to demand the right to rebuild its enrichment program at lower levels, for what it insists are peaceful purposes.

___

Associated Press reporters Michelle L. Price and Matthew Lee in Washington contributed.

news_boston.title: North End restaurant fights city's plan to reinstall bike racks
news_boston.author: Jericho Tran
news_boston.summary:
A restaurant in Boston’s North End is fighting back after being told bike racks would be installed in the middle of its outdoor dining space.

Carla Gomes, the owner and president of Terramia Ristorante, says there were racks — and rats — in the location before. She shared video from three years ago in which dozens of rats can be seen on a planter and rolling around on the wheels of parked bicycles outside her restaurant.

Since then, Boston removed the racks, and the restaurant has repurposed the space into a patio.

Gomes says more than 300 residents have signed a petition asking the city not to reinstall the racks.

“Why do you want to take away a beautiful patio and reinstall bike racks that were constantly vandalized?” she asked.

The restaurant’s outdoor dining permit legally covers only part of the space. Boston sent Gomes a letter last week informing her of the plan to reinstall the bike racks in the middle of the patio.

“They just said, ‘Dismantle your patio, we’re coming in to install bike racks.’ And they gave us no inclination of it, because we’ve been calling them because we submitted a permit to extend our outdoor dining.”

In November 2025, Gomes says she submitted two plans to the city and the Boston Transportation Department. Plan B completely removed the bike racks. She received an answer in May.

“They said Plan B was approved by City Hall, but not approved by the BTD, and they had final approval, and they wanted to put the bike racks back,” she said.

She added that she’s tried to reason with the department and the city to keep the outdoor dining space, which she maintains throughout the year.

“I’m not against bike racks at all,” she said.

The city says it’s willing to allow Gomes to use the space, but still requires a small area for bike racks.

“This proposal built on the City’s continued efforts to work collaboratively with Terramia, including past accommodations as well as the restaurant’s prior agreement to host bicycle rack parking on this site,” a city spokesperson said in a statement.

news_boston.title: Sons sue Boston assisted living facility, claiming father's body was left to rot
news_boston.author: Asher Klein and Griffin Gonzalez
news_boston.summary:
Two men say in a new lawsuit that their father’s death at Boston assisted living facility went unnoticed for over a week, and his body was badly decomposed by the time it was discovered.

The sons of Felipe Dip say the Brighton facility, Chestnut Park at Cleveland Circle, and a staffer in charge of Dip’s welfare were negligent for not checking on the man, resulting in severe emotional distress. The civil lawsuit, filed Tuesday in Suffolk Superior Court, seeks $1 million in damages.

A lawyer for Dip’s sons, Ricardo Dip Calimano and Benjamin Dip Gerber, said they filed the lawsuit for transparency and to hold people accountable.

“Mr. Dip was entrusted to the care of an assisted living facility that families rely on to keep their loved ones safe. The allegations in this case raise serious questions about whether that trust was honored,” attorney Thomas E. Flaws said.

Court documents state that Dip was found in his room at the facility on July 21, 2023, his body decomposed. His sons claim the room’s motion detector last recorded movement on July 12, which they argue was the last day their father was alive.

Now they’re wondering why no one in the living facility took notice of the absence and conducted a welfare check.

Neighbors told NBC10 Boston off camera that they couldn’t believe a body could sit for nine days at an assisted living facility without anyone noticing.

A spokesperson for Benchmark Senior Living, which operates the facility, said it disputes the way the lawsuit characterized what happened.

“The health and safety of our residents is our top priority. Though we cannot comment fully on the merits of active litigation, we strongly disagree with the description of the events and allegations contained in the lawsuit and will defend against them,” the spokesperson said in a statement.

Read the full statement from Flaws:

  • “The family of Felipe Dip has filed this lawsuit to learn the truth about what happened to their father and to hold those responsible accountable. Mr. Dip was entrusted to the care of an assisted living facility that families rely on to keep their loved ones safe. The allegations in this case raise serious questions about whether that trust was honored. Our focus now is on a thorough and transparent examination of the facts. The family deserves answers, and we intend to pursue them through the legal process.”

news_boston.title: Upset with bus lane proposal, Boston residents appeal to Trump admin.
news_boston.author: Matt Prichard
news_boston.summary:
Blue Hill Avenue is among the busiest stretches in Boston. Cars, buses, pedestrians and cyclists all come together along the crowded corridor, with Massachusetts officials and city leaders working towards a revamp.

“How to improve, beautify and speed up,” said Boston Mayor Michelle Wu.

Boston and the MBTA proposed a center bus lane and secured $80 million in federal funds this March toward the $162 million project.

As a result, some in the community are turning to unlikely ally to stall the plan.

“We’re not going to just sit here and let you turn this whole community upside down,” said former Massachusetts State Sen. Dianne Wilkerson.

“He is still our president, we still have the right under the constitution to air our grievances,” said Reggie Stewart with the Boston NAACP.

The Coalition for Responsible Transportation Policy penned a letter to the Trump administration calling on Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy to withdraw the money, writing, “We don’t want this!”

“If the city won’t listen, we’re hoping that the federal government will,” said Wilkerson.

“The MBTA and all the public transportation officials do see a center-running lane as the way to most efficiently bring about those transportation improvements,” Wu said.

The letter and strategy come with inherent risk. The Trump administration could potentially pull back the funds entirely from Blue Hill Avenue.

“The only way we get to accept it is through the likes of a single-lane bus that tears this community apart? They can keep it,” said Wilkerson.

Meanwhile, Wu says the city and transportation officials remain committed to community engagement.

“This is now, really, block-by-block feedback,” she said.

The letter has been received by the Department of Transportation, but there has been no word on whether Duffy will choose to act.

news_boston.title: E-bike operator killed in crash with dump truck in Manchester
news_boston.summary:
A man on an e-bike was hit and killed by a dump truck Wednesday afternoon in Manchester, New Hampshire.

Police responded shortly after 1 p.m. to South Mammoth Road. The truck and the bike were both traveling southbound north of Bodwell Road at the time of the crash.

The e-bike operator, a 31-year-old Londonderry man, was pronounced dead at the scene. His name was not immediately released.

The driver of the dump truck was not hurt.

The cause of the crash is under investigation. Anyone with information is asked to call 603-668-8711.

news_boston.title: Knicks set to be first NBA team to visit White House under Trump
news_boston.author: Kyla Guilfoil | NBC News
news_boston.summary:
The New York Knicks will visit the White House to celebrate their NBA championship, an administration official confirmed to NBC News on Wednesday.

President Donald Trump’s hometown team will be the first NBA champions to visit the White House during either of his terms in office.

Knicks owner James Dolan first shared the news in an interview with WFAN sports radio Wednesday.

“We just did receive an invitation from the White House, which we accepted,” Dolan said. “We still have to figure out the details, etc., but yes, of course.”

A White House official told NBC News on Wednesday that administration officials have “been in touch with the Knicks and look forward to hosting the team at a date to be determined in the near future.”

New York Knicks Jun 16

Knicks celebrate Finals win on ‘Tonight Show,' raise Fallon's lucky game shoe to the rafters

New York Knicks Jun 16

Jalen Brunson, Josh Hart, & Mikal Bridges reveal who's their most surreal celebrity encounter at MSG

Dolan on Wednesday touted his relationship with Trump, whom he invited to attend Game 3 of the NBA Finals at Madison Square Garden on June 8.

“Look, I invited the president to come down for the game. He is a friend. I’ve known him for 30 years, and I’m very proud to bring the team to the White House,” Dolan said.

Trump was booed at the game, the only one the Knicks lost to the San Antonio Spurs during the five-game series.

He watched the game from Dolan’s box, where he was joined by several administration officials, including Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, White House deputy chief of staff Dan Scavino and Environmental Protection Agency Administrator Lee Zeldin.

Trump celebrated the Knicks’ championship early Sunday on Truth Social, congratulating Dolan and top players Jalen Brunson, Karl-Anthony Towns, OG Anunoby and Mitchell Robinson.

“What a year it has been but, even more so, what incredible playoff wins we have all witnessed, especially the last four — Maybe the greatest in the history of basketball,” he wrote.

The Knicks are expected to visit the White House after NBA championship-winning teams skipped the meeting for years, starting with the Golden State Warriors in 2017.

Star player Stephen Curry said in September 2017 that he was not interested in visiting Trump’s White House, sparking a furious social media post from Trump.

“Going to the White House is considered a great honor for a championship team. Stephen Curry is hesitating, therefore invitation is withdrawn!” Trump wrote on X in September 2017.

The Warriors did not visit the White House the next year after they won the championship again. The Toronto Raptors did not attend the White House after their 2019 win, either.

In 2020, the Los Angeles Lakers’ championship win was delayed by the Covid-19 pandemic, and the team did not visit the White House. However, Trump told reporters in 2018 he would not invite Lakers player LeBron James to the White House after James stood behind Curry’s criticisms.

When Trump returned to office last year, the Oklahoma City Thunder said they would not visit the White House after their championship win because of “timing” issues.

Three teams visited during President Joe Biden’s term — the Milwaukee Bucks, the Warriors and the Boston Celtics in 2021, 2023 and 2024, respectively.
